Key AI centers: Center for Artificial Intelligence Learning (AI literacy across campus), Empathetic AI for Health Institute (AI.Health — ML and big data for healthcare transformation), and multiple departmental AI labs. Research spans NLP, computer vision, geospatial AI, health informatics, and AI ethics. Interdisciplinary approach connects AI research with humanities, social sciences, and public health.

name: Emory AI Group
notes: Research collective within Emory University encompassing multiple AI-focused labs and faculty across the Department of Computer Science and School of Medicine. Includes the Emory NLP group, AI and Simulation Lab (geospatial AI, computer vision, foundation models), MAIX Lab (health AI), and Cardiothoracic Imaging and AI Lab. Faculty research spans natural language processing, large language models, conversational AI, information retrieval, AI interpretability, and medical imaging AI. Part of the broader AI.Humanity initiative at Emory.

name: Block Center for Technology and Society
notes: The Block Center for Technology and Society at Carnegie Mellon University was established in 2019 to study how emerging technologies alter the future of work, how AI and analytics can be harnessed for social good, and how innovation can reduce inequality. Houses the Responsible AI Initiative (RAI), which translates AI research into actionable policy insights promoting fairness, accountability, and transparency. Runs community education programs like 'AI 101' for labor unions, media professionals, and government representatives. Collaborates with MIT FutureTech on labor implications of AI research. Engages directly with policymakers in Washington, D.C., sharing AI expertise. Research focuses on future of work, responsible AI deployment, and data-driven policy solutions.

name: Manish Parashar
notes: Presidential Professor at the University of Utah's Kahlert School of Computing and Director of the Scientific Computing and Imaging (SCI) Institute. Appointed as the university's inaugural Chief AI Officer. Led the launch of the One-U Responsible AI Initiative with $100 million investment in AI research across environment, healthcare, and education. Previously served at the National Science Foundation as Office Director of Advanced Cyberinfrastructure, co-chaired the National Science and Technology Council's Subcommittee on Future Advanced Computing Ecosystem and the National AI Research Resource (NAIRR) Task Force. Won the 2023 IEEE Sidney Fernbach Award and 2024 CRA Distinguished Service Award. Research spans parallel/distributed computing and computational science.

name: Amii
notes: The Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute (Amii), one of Canada's three national AI institutes alongside Mila and Vector Institute, funded through the Pan-Canadian AI Strategy. Founded over 20 years ago, headquartered in Edmonton. Mandate to build technical/scientific capacity in machine learning and translate it into commercial applications across Alberta's economic sectors. Key partner in the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI) alongside CIFAR, NRC, Mila, and Vector. Received $5M Google.org grant to establish a national AI literacy consortium across 25 post-secondary institutions reaching 125,000 students. Also received $9M federal grant to train ~5,000 mid-career energy sector workers in AI skills. Strong focus on AI trust and safety, workforce readiness, and responsible AI deployment.

name: Jean-François Gagné
notes: Co-founder and former CEO of Element AI, a Montreal-based applied AI research lab co-founded with Yoshua Bengio and Nicolas Chapados. Element AI was acquired by ServiceNow in 2020 for ~$500M. Subsequently served as Head of AI Product Management and Strategy at ServiceNow. As of 2025-2026, working on a stealth AI startup based in Montreal. Recognized as one of Fast Company's Most Creative People in Business (2019). Active voice in Canada's AI ecosystem, advocating for AI commercialization and startup development. Element AI was a notable early attempt to bridge academic AI research and enterprise applications.

name: ServiceNow
notes: Public enterprise software company (NYSE: NOW) specializing in workflow automation. Heavily invested in AI via 'Now Assist' and autonomous AI agents; targeting $1B AI ACV by 2026. Advocates responsible AI governance and compliance with EU AI Act and NIST AI RMF.

name: Government of Canada
notes: Canada was the first country globally to adopt a national AI strategy, launching the Pan-Canadian AI Strategy in 2017. Phase 2 announced in 2022 with $443M+ in funding, supporting the three national AI institutes (Mila, Amii, Vector). Tabled the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) in June 2022 as part of Bill C-27, one of the first proposed AI-specific laws globally. AIDA focuses on responsible design, development, and use of AI in the private sector, with emphasis on health, safety, and human rights impacts. Requires businesses to implement governance mechanisms, assess AI risks, and provide transparency to users. Established the Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI). Also investing in AI workforce development, compute infrastructure, and commercialization support across provinces.

name: Data Collective
notes: DCVC (formerly Data Collective) is a deep tech venture capital firm founded by Matt Ocko and Zachary Bogue, investing in entrepreneurs using computational approaches—especially AI—to solve large-scale real-world problems. Manages billions in AUM across 13 funds including six flagship funds. Invests at seed, Series A, and growth stages across AI, exascale computing, climate, robotics, materials science, biology, defense, and security. Backed by a network of 50+ technology executives and experts (CTOs, chief scientists, professors at Stanford and Berkeley). Was a lead investor in Element AI. Focus on AI-enabled solutions for industries that haven't seen material progress in decades.

name: Real Ventures
notes: Canada's leading early-stage venture capital firm, established in 2007 and headquartered in Montreal. Manages $325M across five funds with an active portfolio of 100+ companies valued at ~$10B. Has invested in 214 companies total, with portfolio unicorns including Xanadu, Blockstream, and Tenstorrent. Deeply embedded in Montreal's AI and deep tech ecosystem. Was an early investor/backer of Element AI. Focuses on nurturing entrepreneurial communities and supporting founders from the earliest stages. Portfolio spans AI, healthcare, fintech, and other technology verticals. Sonder (portfolio company) went public on NASDAQ in 2021.

name: Amnesty International
notes: Major international human rights organization with strong positions on AI governance and digital rights. Calls for outright ban on facial recognition technology for mass surveillance by police and state agencies, citing racial discrimination (higher false positive rates for women and people of color) and chilling effects on peaceful assembly. Runs the 'Ban the Scan' campaign documenting FRT harms in New York, Israel/OPT, and Hyderabad. Advocates for legally binding AI regulation centered on documented harms, not voluntary industry commitments. Criticized the EU AI Act as insufficient to protect marginalized communities. Launched an Algorithmic Accountability Toolkit (2025) for investigators and rights defenders. Calls for meaningful involvement of impacted communities in AI regulatory decisions and warns against regulatory gaps that allow export of banned AI systems to other countries.

name: Singapore Management University
notes: Singapore Management University (SMU) hosts the Centre for AI and Data Governance, established in 2019 at its School of Law. Received a S$4.5M grant from the National Research Foundation and Infocomm Media Development Authority for a five-year Research Programme on the Governance of AI and Data Use, structured around three streams: AI and Society, AI and Industry, and AI and Commercialisation. Publishes research on Singapore's evolving AI governance framework through SSRN. The Centre establishes expert panels drawing from academia, technology industry, and legal professions, with international institutional partnerships. Also offers AI-led business strategy programs. Contributing to Singapore's national approach to responsible AI and data governance.

name: Apple
notes: Consumer technology company deploying AI via Apple Intelligence across its device ecosystem. Pursues an on-device, privacy-first AI strategy using Private Cloud Compute for sensitive tasks. Partnered with OpenAI (ChatGPT integration) and Google (Gemini for Siri). Avoids public regulatory advocacy but leverages privacy standards as a competitive differentiator.

name: Aaron Courville
notes: Full Professor at Université de Montréal and founding core member of Mila. Co-authored the seminal Deep Learning textbook with Bengio and Goodfellow. Holds a CIFAR AI Chair. Incoming scientific director of IVADO as of 2025.

name: McGill Centre for Intelligent Machines
notes: Established in 1985 as an inter-departmental, inter-faculty research center at McGill University spanning Electrical and Computer Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, and Computer Science. One of the longest-running AI research centers in the world. Comprises ~20 professors and 150 graduate students. Research organized around five themes: Computer Vision, Robotics, Systems and Control, Human-Computer Interaction, and Graphics. Half of CIM professors engaged in AI research including robotic vision and medical image analysis. Notable projects include the Aqua amphibious robot for environmental monitoring and the SmartWheeler assistive technology. Draws researchers from around the world to McGill.

name: McGill Collaborative for AI and Society
notes: The McGill Collaborative for AI and Society (McCAIS) is a strategic initiative of the Computational and Data Systems Institute (CDSI) at McGill University. Interdisciplinary, university-wide effort bringing all faculties together to understand AI's societal impact and develop evidence-based best practices for responsible AI use. Work organized around four pillars: Thought Leadership, Research to Action, Experiential Learning Opportunities, and Lifelong Learning. Partners with IVADO (Quebec AI research consortium) on courses in algorithmic fairness, privacy in AI, creativity in AI, and AI in health sciences. Runs the BMO Responsible AI Awards Program. Draws expertise from Philosophy, Engineering, Computer Science, and the Laidley Centre for Business Ethics and Equity. Co-directed by Prof. Samira A. Rahimi.

name: McGill AI Lab
notes: Umbrella term for McGill University's AI research labs, most prominently the Reasoning and Learning Lab co-directed by Prakash Panangaden, Doina Precup, Joelle Pineau, Jackie Chi Kit Cheung, Siva Reddy, Reihaneh Rabbany, Siamak Ravanbakhsh, and David Rolnick. McGill is a world leader in machine learning with particular strengths in reinforcement learning and natural language processing. Connected to Mila (Quebec AI Institute) through shared faculty. The iSMART Lab (Prof. Narges Armanfard) focuses on computer vision, time series analysis, and physics-informed modeling. Also participates in the International Laboratory on Learning Systems (ILLS) with ETS, CNRS, Universite Paris-Saclay, and CentraleSupelec. Closely linked to CIM (Centre for Intelligent Machines) and McCAIS within McGill's AI ecosystem.

name: Mark Nitzberg
notes: Executive Director of the Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI) at UC Berkeley since 2016, and Interim Executive Director of IASEAI. Also head of strategic outreach for Berkeley AI Research (BAIR). Former CEO of The Blindsight Corporation (acquired by Amazon A9).

name: Amir Banifatemi
notes: Chief Responsible AI Officer at Cognizant. Former GM of Innovation at XPRIZE, led IBM Watson AI XPRIZE. Co-founded AI Commons collective. Co-created AI for Good Global Summit with ITU/UN. Elected to GPAI Multistakeholder Expert Group, co-chairs Responsible AI working group. 25+ years in emerging tech, prior roles at European Space Agency, Airbus, European Commission. Managed two venture capital funds.

name: OECD
notes: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (38 member countries). Published OECD AI Principles in 2019 (first intergovernmental AI standard), updated 2024 — now endorsed by 47 jurisdictions. Operates AI Policy Observatory (oecd.ai) tracking AI strategies, policies, and incidents globally. Integrated GPAI partnership under OECD umbrella (44 countries). Partners with UN Office of Tech Envoy. Published common AI incident reporting framework (Feb 2025). Member-funded intergovernmental org.

name: Joanna Rodriguez
notes: Communications Director at National Republican Senatorial Committee (NRSC) since Jan 2025. Championed NRSC use of AI in political campaigns, defended AI-generated campaign ads including deepfake-style ad targeting Chuck Schumer (Oct 2025). Stated "AI is here and not going anywhere. Adapt & win or pearl clutch & lose." Previously worked in office of Sen. Pete Ricketts.

name: Public Investment Fund
notes: Saudi Arabia sovereign wealth fund (~$930B AUM). Launched Humain (May 2025) as full-stack AI company — data centers, cloud, models (ALLAM Arabic LLM), applications. $40B AI investment fund. Strategic partnership with NVIDIA (500MW GPU capacity over 5 years). $10B joint investment with Google Cloud for AI hub. Aramco acquired minority stake in Humain. Central to Vision 2030 economic diversification. Chaired by Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.

name: Mohammed bin Salman
notes: Crown Prince and Prime Minister of Saudi Arabia. Architect of Vision 2030, which places AI as central pillar of economic diversification. Chairs Humain (PIF-backed AI company). Established Saudi Data & AI Authority (SDAIA) in 2019. National Strategy for Data & AI targets 20K AI specialists, 300+ AI startups, $20B investment by 2030. Designated 2026 as Year of Artificial Intelligence. Oversaw $40B+ in AI infrastructure commitments.

name: National Infrastructure Fund
notes: Saudi Arabian infrastructure financing entity operating under the National Development Fund. Signed $1.2B financing framework with Humain (announced at Davos, Jan 2026) to develop 250MW of AI data center capacity. Exploring creation of AI data center investment platform to attract global/local institutional investors for Humain AI strategy. Supports Vision 2030 tech infrastructure goals.

name: Michael J. Berry
notes: Professor at Princeton University, Department of Molecular Biology and Princeton Neuroscience Institute. Runs the Berry Lab studying neural coding in the retina. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) during doctoral research on large-scale electrophysiology of neural circuits. Published extensively on neural population coding and information processing in biological systems.

name: William Bialek
notes: Theoretical biophysicist, professor at Princeton University (Physics) and CUNY Graduate Center. Pioneer in applying information theory to neural coding and biological systems. Co-advised Dario Amodei (Anthropic CEO) on PhD dissertation "Network-Scale Electrophysiology: Measuring and Understanding the Collective Behavior of Neural Circuits." Co-authored PNAS paper on thermodynamics and criticality in neural networks with Amodei.

name: Arizona Secretary of State
notes: Office held by Adrian Fontes (D). Formed AI & Election Security Advisory Committee (June 2024), co-chaired by Chris Cummiskey and Gowri Ramachandran. Released final report (Feb 2025) proposing AI Elections Lab for developing guidelines and pilot programs. Deployed AI tools in 2024 election: chatbot handled 655K+ interactions, SMS system reached 281K voters. Advocates both beneficial AI use in elections and safeguards against AI-driven threats. Pushes for state and federal AI election policy.

name: Mark Chen
notes: Chief Research Officer at OpenAI. Joined 2018 after working as quantitative trader at Jane Street Capital. Led development of Codex (adapting GPT-3 for code generation), co-led vision team for GPT-4 (image recognition integration), contributed to DALL-E. Advocates focusing on "main quest" of AGI over short-term product competition. Views math/coding proficiency as "bedrock for a far more general form of intelligence." Background in competitive programming.

name: Colorado AI Policy Work Group
notes: Convened by Governor Jared Polis (Oct 2025) to revise Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205), the nation's first comprehensive AI anti-discrimination law. Diverse membership: consumer groups, hospitals, school districts, tech companies large and small. Delivered unanimous support for revised policy framework (March 2026) emphasizing transparency over bias audits: up-front notice when AI is used in consequential decisions, access to information on adverse decisions, right to correction, human review option. Recommendations to be codified in legislation.

name: Greg Brockman
notes: Co-founder and President of OpenAI. Took a sabbatical Aug–Nov 2024, then returned. In 2025 led OpenAI's Stargate infrastructure buildout. Major individual backer of the pro-AI super PAC 'Leading the Future,' launched August 2025 by AI-industry figures (including Andreessen Horowitz partners and Joe Lonsdale) to oppose state-level AI regulation and shape the 2026 midterms; the PAC raised $125M+ in 2025, with Brockman and his wife each contributing $12.5M.

name: Helion Energy
notes: Private nuclear fusion company building the Orion plant in Chelan County, WA, targeting power delivery to Microsoft data centers by 2028 under the world's first fusion power purchase agreement. Backed by Sam Altman ($350M+) and SoftBank; raised $425M Series F in Jan 2025 at a $5.4B valuation. Directly tied to AI compute energy demands.

name: Ben Mann
notes: Co-founder of Anthropic. Previously architect of GPT-3 at OpenAI. Left OpenAI in 2020 with seven colleagues to found Anthropic because safety was not the top priority. Advocates layered safety approach ("Swiss Cheese Model of Defense"). Key contributor to Constitutional AI methodology. Estimates 50th percentile chance of superintelligence by ~2028. Describes alignment challenge as "how do we keep God in a box."

name: Clay Bavor
notes: Co-founder of Sierra AI (with Bret Taylor), an enterprise AI agent platform valued at $10B. Former Google VP (18 years), led Google Labs, AR/VR, Project Starline, Google Lens. Sierra hit $100M ARR in 7 quarters. AI optimist focused on applied/enterprise AI.

name: Eleos AI
notes: Nonprofit research org focused on AI welfare, consciousness, and moral patienthood. Co-founded by Kyle Fish, Robert Long, Kathleen Finlinson. Published landmark report "Taking AI Welfare Seriously." Commended Anthropic's model welfare efforts. Five research priorities: welfare interventions, human-AI cooperation, leveraging AI for welfare research, standardized evaluations, credible communication.

name: David Chalmers
notes: University Professor of Philosophy and Neural Science at NYU. Co-director of Center for Mind, Brain, and Consciousness. Formulated the "hard problem of consciousness." Leading voice on AI consciousness and moral status. Author of "Reality+" on virtual worlds. Warns against corporate control of virtual/AI worlds.

name: Robert Long
notes: Executive Director of Eleos AI. PhD Philosophy from NYU. Former research fellow at Future of Humanity Institute (Oxford) and CAIS Philosophy Fellow (2023). Leading researcher on AI consciousness, sentience, and moral status. Co-author of "Consciousness in Artificial Intelligence" with Butlin, Bengio, et al. NOTE: Same person as Rob Long (entity 1354) — "Rob" is informal name.

name: Kathleen Finlinson
notes: Head of Strategy and co-founder of Eleos AI. Background in applied mathematics, data science, and AI forecasting. Researches strategic considerations for AI welfare action. Advocates balancing AI control with cooperative strategies that respect AI welfare.

name: Niskanen Center
notes: Centrist/center-right think tank founded 2015 by Jerry Taylor (ex-Cato). Named "Most Interesting Think Tank" by TIME (2023). AI policy stance: regulatory restraint, intervene only when harm is realistic. Has 501(c)(3) research arm and 501(c)(4) lobbying arm. Major funders include Hewlett Foundation, Open Philanthropy, Carnegie Corp.

name: Federation of American Scientists
notes: DC-based science and technology policy nonprofit founded in 1945. FAS runs the Day One Project to translate scientific expertise into federal policy; active in AI governance, advocating for responsible AI innovation, NIST AISI funding, and federal AI adoption. Submitted AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House in 2025.

name: U.S. Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute
notes: Housed within NIST. Develops science-based metrics, tools, and guidelines for AI safety evaluation. Established by Biden EO 14110 (Oct 2023). Paul Christiano served as Head of AI Safety. Runs AISIC consortium (290+ members). Renamed to Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) in 2025. Budget ~$10M (2024). NOTE: Related entities — 205 (U.S. AI Safety Institute at NIST, likely same entity), 1629 (AISIC consortium, its membership body), 1309 (NIST, parent agency).

name: Model Evaluation and Threat Research
notes: Nonprofit AI safety eval org (formerly ARC Evals, spun off Dec 2023). Founded by Beth Barnes (ex-OpenAI). Evaluates frontier model capabilities for catastrophic risk. Worked with OpenAI (o3, GPT-4.5) and Anthropic (Claude). Does NOT accept lab funding to avoid conflicts of interest. Budget ~$13-15M/year. Funded via Audacious Project (TED) and donations. Paul Christiano was originally involved but stepped back for AISI role.

name: UK government's Frontier AI Taskforce
notes: UK government body established April 2023 with £100m mandate to evaluate frontier AI safety risks ahead of the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit. Chaired by Ian Hogarth, it transitioned in November 2023 into the AI Safety Institute (later renamed AI Security Institute in 2025), which is a separate entity in this database.

name: MIT Jameel Clinic
notes: Abdul Latif Jameel Clinic for Machine Learning in Health, founded 2018 as a joint MIT–Community Jameel initiative. Conducts translational AI research across clinical AI, drug discovery, and epidemiology. Developed clinical AI tools MIRAI and SYBIL, and discovered antibiotics halicin and abaucin using AI-driven methods.

name: MIT Computer Science & Artificial Intelligence Laboratory
notes: MIT's largest AI research lab, directed by Daniela Rus, covering robotics, systems, and machine learning. In 2025 submitted AI Action Plan recommendations to the White House advocating federal investment in basic research, AI hardware co-design, and ASI-level capabilities. Published the 2025 AI Agent Index assessing transparency of deployed AI systems.

name: Cornell University
notes: Major research university with broad AI activities spanning Cornell Tech (NYC), the AI Initiative, and the MacArthur-funded AI, Policy, and Practice (AIPP) initiative focused on AI's societal impacts. In 2025 NSF awarded Cornell a $20M AI Materials Institute, and a new Vice Provost for AI Strategy was named in January 2026.

name: Shane Legg
notes: Co-founder and Chief AGI Scientist at Google DeepMind, leading its Technical AGI Safety team. Has predicted a 50% chance of human-level AGI by 2028 since 2009, reaffirmed publicly in 2025. Advocates for deliberative, human-auditable AI safety mechanisms to address alignment and misuse risks.

name: Mustafa Suleyman
notes: Mustafa Suleyman co-founded DeepMind (2010) and Inflection AI (2022), and became CEO of Microsoft AI in 2024. He argues AI is 'already superhuman' and predicts human-level task performance within 5-7 years. He advocates for humanist superintelligence with strong human control, global regulations, and has pledged to halt development if AI poses existential risk.

name: John M. Jumper
notes: Director at Google DeepMind. Led development of AlphaFold (protein structure prediction). Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 (shared with Demis Hassabis and David Baker). PhD theoretical chemistry (U of Chicago). AI optimist — believes AI has "unparalleled potential to improve the lives of billions."

name: Black in AI
notes: Nonprofit founded 2017 by Timnit Gebru and Rediet Abebe. Increases presence and inclusion of Black people in AI. Four pillars: education pathways, financial barriers, visibility, and community support. Received MacArthur Foundation funding. Hosts annual workshop at NeurIPS. Aims to broaden voices in AI development, deployment, and regulation.

name: Margaret Mitchell
notes: Chief Ethics Scientist at Hugging Face. Previously founded and co-led Google Ethical AI group (fired 2021 alongside Timnit Gebru). Pioneered "Model Cards" for ML model reporting. Created "Seeing AI" for blind/low-vision users. ~100 papers on NLG, assistive tech, CV, AI ethics. Strong advocate for diversity in AI and regulation that centers those most harmed.

name: Meredith Stiehm
notes: President of WGA West (2021-2025). TV showrunner (The Rookie, Code Black, Cold Case, NYPD Blue). Named TIME 100 Most Influential People in AI 2024. Led WGA during historic 148-day strike in 2023 that secured precedent-setting AI protections for writers. Called on studios to sue AI companies for using copyrighted work to train models. Strong advocate that AI cannot replace human creative experience.

name: Lisa Takeuchi Cullen
notes: President of WGA East (elected Sept 2023). First person of color to hold the position. TV writer/producer (Empire, Five Days at Memorial). Participated in April 2024 White House roundtable on AI and Workers Rights with Acting Secretary of Labor Julie Su. Launched WGA East AI Task Force to track AI's effect on writing professions. Strong enforcement of 2023 MBA AI protections for film/TV/streaming writers.

name: Club for Growth Action
notes: Conservative super PAC affiliated with Club for Growth. Supports limited-government, free-market candidates. Major donors include Peter Thiel and Richard Uihlein. Focuses on tax cuts, deregulation, free trade, school choice. No specific AI policy position — general deregulatory stance would extend to opposing AI regulation. Founded 2010 as independent expenditure arm.

name: University of Notre Dame
notes: Private Catholic research university in Indiana. Major AI initiatives: AI@ND hub, Lucy Family Institute for Data & Society, Scientific AI Initiative. Received $50.8M Lilly Endowment grant for DELTA Network expanding faith-based AI ethics framework. Won $1M Bloomberg Mayors Challenge Award for human-centered AI in public service with City of South Bend. Pete Buttigieg is an alumnus.

name: Molly Kinder
notes: Senior Fellow at Brookings Institution, leading multi-year project on generative AI's impact on work and workers. Nationally recognized expert on AI, economic inequality, and future of work. Advocates for worker voice in AI implementation, enhanced union protections, and public investment in workforce training. Argues U.S. lags Europe in institutional mechanisms to help workers navigate AI transition. Work cited by Sen. Chris Murphy.

name: Munich Security Conference
notes: Premier annual international security conference held in Munich, Germany (since 1963). Increasingly prominent on AI governance. Convened 'Tech Accord to Combat Deceptive Use of AI in 2024 Elections' signed by 27 tech companies. Hosts Frontier AI Safety Commitments discussions. Technology program frames AI as 'decisive factor' in systemic competition. 2025 conference marked shift from AI safety to AI security framing.

name: Joe Biden
notes: 46th U.S. President (2021-2025). Issued Executive Order 14110 in October 2023, the most comprehensive U.S. AI governance action to that point, requiring safety reporting from frontier AI developers and coordinating federal agency standards. Also released the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights in 2022.

name: SpaceX
notes: Aerospace and space launch company that merged with xAI in February 2026 to create a $1.25T 'Orbital Intelligence' entity. Plans space-based AI data centers using Starlink satellite infrastructure. Founder Elon Musk favors light-touch AI regulation while pursuing aggressive AI deployment through xAI's Grok models.

name: X
notes: X (formerly Twitter), owned by Elon Musk, integrates the Grok AI chatbot developed by xAI. In March 2025, xAI formally acquired X in an all-stock deal. X has faced EU and UK regulatory investigations over Grok-generated deepfake images and has deployed content restrictions in response.

name: Neuralink
notes: Brain-computer interface company co-founded by Elon Musk (2016). Developing implantable BCI (N1 chip) for paralysis patients. FDA IDE approval May 2023; first human implant Jan 2024 (PRIME trial). FDA Breakthrough Device Designation for speech restoration tech (May 2025). Raised over $700M. Regulatory gap: neural data privacy laws lag behind technology. Competitor Precision Neuroscience also received FDA clearance.

name: High-Flyer AI
notes: High-Flyer (Zhejiang High-Flyer Asset Management) is a Chinese quantitative hedge fund founded by Liang Wenfeng. In 2023 it incubated DeepSeek as a spin-off AI lab, funding it internally. High-Flyer posted 56.6% average returns in 2025 and manages over 70 billion yuan (~$10B USD).

name: Tejas N. Narechania
notes: Professor of Law at UC Berkeley School of Law. Faculty co-director of Berkeley Center for Law & Technology. Former clerk for Justice Breyer (SCOTUS) and Judge Diane Wood (7th Circuit). Former FCC special counsel (2012-2013). Published 'An Antimonopoly Approach to Governing AI' (Yale Law & Policy Review, 2024) with Ganesh Sitaraman — argues AI infrastructure warrants regulation via structural separation, nondiscrimination, and public cloud options. Work cited in 2024 Economic Report of the President. Participated in White House AI competition policy meeting.

name: Stanford Vision and Learning Lab
notes: Stanford Vision and Learning Lab (SVL), co-directed by Fei-Fei Li, focuses on computer vision, embodied AI, and robotics. Key projects include BEHAVIOR benchmark for household AI agents and the PAIR group for generalizable robot perception. Li has called for science-based AI governance.

name: AI4ALL
notes: Nonprofit co-founded in 2017 by Fei-Fei Li, Olga Russakovsky, and Rick Sommer to increase diversity in AI. Grew from 2015 Stanford summer program for high school girls. 2022 strategic pivot to College Pathways — accelerating underrepresented college students into AI internships and research. Partnerships with 16+ universities (Texas A&M, UIUC, UMBC). Early funders include Melinda French Gates/Pivotal Ventures and Jensen Huang.

name: Justin Johnson
notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Christoph Lassner, Ben Mildenhall). Assistant Professor of CS at University of Michigan. PhD from Stanford (advised by Fei-Fei Li). Key contributor to early PyTorch development. Created CLEVR visual reasoning benchmark. NSF CAREER Award recipient. World Labs raised $1B+ at $5B valuation, building Large World Models for 3D perception.

name: Christoph Lassner
notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Ben Mildenhall). PhD from University of Tubingen. Previously led research teams at Epic Games and Meta Reality Labs. Created Pulsar differentiable renderer (precursor to Gaussian Splatting). Built human pose estimation for Amazon Halo (via Bodylabs acquisition). Specialist in 3D computer vision and graphics.

name: Ben Mildenhall
notes: Co-founder of World Labs (spatial intelligence startup, founded 2024 with Fei-Fei Li, Justin Johnson, Christoph Lassner). Co-creator of NeRF (Neural Radiance Fields), ECCV 2020 honorable mention for best paper. PhD from UC Berkeley (advised by Ren Ng), Hertz Fellow. Former Google Research scientist (2021-2023). Research spans computational photography, 3D reconstruction, and text-to-3D generation.

name: John Etchemendy
notes: Stanford professor of philosophy and former provost (2000-2017). Co-founded and co-directs Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI (HAI) with Fei-Fei Li since 2019. Leading advocate for national AI research resource to counter industry concentration of AI compute and data. Argues 'the science has outgrown the university model of research.' HAI promotes human-centered approach: AI evaluation and accountability over hype. Expert in logic and philosophy of language.

name: Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences
notes: The primary academic division of Harvard University housing most arts, humanities, and sciences departments. Home to the Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence (launched 2022), a university-wide initiative with strong FAS ties bridging neuroscience and AI. FAS Research Computing supports the Kempner AI cluster.

name: Ford Foundation
notes: Major US philanthropy (est. 1936) with significant AI accountability and governance funding. Part of $200M+ coalition (with MacArthur, Omidyar, Mozilla, Open Society Foundations) for public-interest AI. Co-launched Humanity AI, a $500M five-year initiative (Oct 2025) with ten major foundations. Funds AI Now Institute. Supports efforts on AI racial/social bias accountability, labor rights in AI deployment, and global AI fairness standards through Current AI collaboration. Focus areas: transparency, algorithmic accountability, worker protections.

name: Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur
notes: Premier Indian technical university (est. 1959). Established Wadhwani School of Advanced AI and Intelligent Systems with Department of Intelligent Systems (2025), admissions starting 2026. Hosts Sustainable Cities Centre of Excellence in AI under Ministry of Education initiative. Research partnerships with TCS (urban planning), IBM (air quality monitoring via AIRAWAT Foundation). Active in AI for sustainable cities, robotics, cybersecurity. Growing AI policy and outreach center.

name: Trust::Data Alliance
notes: MIT-based consortium (Trust::Data Consortium) founded by Alex 'Sandy' Pentland. Builds open-source tools for secure, privacy-preserving data sharing using blockchain and AI. Mission: create trusted internet-based network for safe data exchange. Focus areas: data cooperatives, privacy-preserving identity systems, safe distributed computation. Advocates collective data governance model over individualized data control. Part of MIT Connection Science ecosystem.

name: Scott Alexander
notes: Blogger and writer behind Astral Codex Ten (formerly Slate Star Codex). Psychiatrist by training. Arguably the most influential AI forecasting voice in the rationalist/EA community. Co-authored 'AI 2027' scenario analysis (April 2025) with Daniel Kokotajlo, modeling fast intelligence explosion by 2027-2028. Supports AI safety research funding (ACX Grants). Nuanced on regulation: supports safety standards but worries about US-China race dynamics leading to worst-case policy tradeoffs. Personal AGI median later in 2020s/early 2030s.

name: Thomas Larsen
notes: Researcher at the AI Futures Project focused on dangerous capability evaluations, AI safety measurement, and compute governance. Co-authored the AI 2027 scenario forecast with Daniel Kokotajlo and Eli Lifland. Previously founded the Center for AI Policy; also a MATS mentor.

name: Eli Lifland
notes: Founding Researcher at AI Futures Project and leading AI forecaster; co-author of the AI 2027 scenario (April 2025) alongside Daniel Kokotajlo, Thomas Larsen, and Romeo Dean. Previously forecasted at Samotsvety and worked at Ought and SaferAI. Co-founded Sage (a forecasting platform) and has served as guest fund manager at the Long Term Future Fund. Publicly argues that short AGI timelines are underweighted and that existential risk from AI is not taken seriously enough.

name: Romeo Dean
notes: Researcher at AI Futures Project and co-author of the AI 2027 scenario forecast (April 2025). Graduated cum laude from Harvard with a concurrent AB/SM in Computer Science; previously an AI Policy Fellow at the Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (IAPS) and an Astra Fellow at Constellation. Specializes in forecasting AI chip production, government intervention, and international coordination dynamics. Also a mentor in the MATS (Machine Learning Alignment Theory Scholars) program.

name: University of Tuebingen
notes: German research university and major European AI hub. Home to Tübingen AI Center (co-directed by Philipp Hennig and Matthias Bethge since July 2025) and Cluster of Excellence 'Machine Learning: New Perspectives for Science' (renewed 2025). Partners with Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, ELLIS Institute Tübingen, and Cyber Valley ecosystem. ML Cloud serves 430+ active researchers. Focus areas: machine learning, computer vision, robotics, probabilistic methods.

name: Philipp Hennig
notes: Chair for Methods of Machine Learning at University of Tübingen. Co-director of Tübingen AI Center since July 2025. Pioneer of probabilistic numerics — treating numerical computation as machine learning inference. Author of 'Probabilistic Numerics: Computation as Machine Learning' (Cambridge University Press, 2022). Previously at Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems. Organizer of ProbNum conference series since 2012. Over 11,000 citations.
